# OQS status meeting – 2026-05-12 – summary

*Note: Generated by AI based on the agenda and transcript.*

## Attendees

- Bruce Xu (University of Waterloo)
- Douglas Stebila (University of Waterloo)
- Edwin Makiuchi (Cisco)
- Mark Albert (Cisco)
- Michael Baentsch
- Norman Ashley (Cisco)
- Rodrigo Martín (Indra)

**Date and time:** Tuesday, May 12, 2026, 10:00–10:30 AM US Eastern / 7:00–7:30 AM US Pacific / 16:00–16:30 Central European

**Location:** Zoom

Information about the OQS project is available at [https://openquantumsafe.org](https://openquantumsafe.org). The project dashboard is at [https://openquantumsafe.org/dashboard.html](https://openquantumsafe.org/dashboard.html).

## OQS Technical Steering Committee

### Conferences and outreach (issues [#276](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/276), [#277](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/277))

Rodrigo, who is currently at Eurocrypt in Rome, was approached by Nadim Kobeissi about a possible OQS speaking slot at Cedar Crypt — a new Eurocrypt-style event in Cyprus in July targeted at grad students and early master's students. The group discussed how to prioritize conference attendance given limited resources. Michael suggested ranking the candidate venues, with the NIST standardization conference at the top because it would put OQS in front of the people building these algorithms.

Rodrigo will give a preliminary ranking of the venues on the issue (building on Michael's earlier ranking) and invited others to weigh in. Rodrigo also thinks his company would be able to cover travel for at least the initial high-priority conferences (OpenSSL, PKI Consortium, NIST standardization). Douglas noted he had emailed Hart after the TSC meeting flagging that the TSC expects PQCA / Linux Foundation budget support for this, which Rodrigo and Michael welcomed.

Separately, Rodrigo argued OQS should not wait for conferences to make contact with the round-2 on-ramp signature scheme teams — the project should proactively reach out, mirroring what OQS did before the first NIST standardization process. Michael agreed and tied this in to Douglas's draft outreach message for contacting upstreams of currently-integrated algorithms (see the algorithm support level discussion below).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Update the venues issue with a preliminary ranking; start outreach to the round-2 on-ramp signature scheme teams (independently of the TSC meeting cadence).

### PR [#269](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/pull/269) — Update liboqs permissions

Douglas flagged PR [#269](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/pull/269), which updates the TSC permissions config to reflect the recently-merged liboqs `GOVERNANCE.md` / `CODEOWNERS` changes. Per TSC practice it needs two TSC member approvals; Rodrigo approved it just before the meeting and Douglas asked other TSC members to take a look.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (TSC members):** Review and approve PR [#269](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/pull/269).

### Security mailing list

Michael asked whether Rodrigo is on the OQS security mailing list (he isn't, and neither he nor Douglas is sure of the process for adding someone). Michael raised this because another security issue report has come in that did not get a timely response. Michael looked at it and believes it is not serious (assembly code, sufficient bytes), but the lack of any response on the list is the concern. Douglas will look into how the list is administered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Find out how the OQS security mailing list is administered and get Rodrigo added.

## liboqs

### 0.16.0 release planning

Douglas summarized that the two outstanding PRs blocking 0.16.0 are the HQC implementation update and the algorithm support levels table. Michael shared a link to the milestone collection as the canonical list of items to check off. Other open PRs on liboqs may land opportunistically but aren't blockers. If HQC review completes in the next week or so, the group expects a release candidate next week and a final release by the end of May.

### HQC update (PR [#2407](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2407))

Bruce reported the HQC PR is ready and waiting on reviews. Rodrigo had already reviewed and was satisfied, modulo the code-duplication concern previously discussed (which stems from the upstream HQC repo only being able to compile one parameter set at a time). Michael noted the PR is 193 files, which is larger than ideal.

Bruce clarified the current structure: liboqs does *not* depend on his personal fork — instead, the integration pulls directly from the official GitLab HQC repo and applies a large patch on top. This sidesteps the upstream/downstream concern, at the cost of being unergonomic for future contributors.

On engaging the upstream HQC team: Bruce has a draft email and was planning to send it that day (and did, during the meeting). Bruce's position is that liboqs should not gate the 0.16.0 release on a response, and can absorb upstream improvements later. Rodrigo offered to approach members of the HQC team in person at Eurocrypt — he has identified that the Eurocrypt general chair is an HQC participant — once Bruce's email has gone out, possibly during an evening social event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all):** Review PR [#2407](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2407).

### Algorithm support levels table (PR [#2413](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2413))

Douglas and Michael have been iterating on review comments; Douglas posted responses last night and Michael confirmed in the meeting that his comments are addressed. The plan after this PR lands is to reach out to each upstream algorithm team using the outreach message in the issue, tracked via GitHub child issues (a feature the project hasn't used before).

Michael raised concern that having lots of "TBD" entries in the table would not read well in a 0.16.0 release. The group converged on: reach out to the upstream teams now, land the table with "TBD — reached out, awaiting response" entries where applicable, and update entries as responses come in — rather than blocking the release on external responses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Merge PR [#2413](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2413) and then create child issues to track outreach to each upstream algorithm team.

### PR [#2416](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2416) — LMS enhancements

Norm flagged in chat that he has opened PR [#2416](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2416) with performance enhancements for LMS and is looking for reviewers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (reviewers):** Review PR [#2416](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2416).

## OQS-OpenSSL provider

Activity has been quiet. The MQOM inclusion PR was merged (thanks to Michael). The other active item is PR [#778](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/778), aimed at limiting the performance impact of elliptic-curve hybrids when encoders are activated — still in draft. Norm is working with the contributor (Vishnith) on the design.

Once HQC lands in liboqs, the group plans to re-enable HQC by default in the provider and cut a provider release aligned with liboqs 0.16.0.

### Security advisories

Rodrigo mentioned the provider received a couple of security reports about a month ago (double-free issues) where the reporter asked whether CVEs would be issued. The group consensus is that a CVE is not warranted for issues at this severity (double-free, not exploitable to a serious level).

## OQS-OpenSSH

Douglas reported that PR [#194](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/openssh/pull/194) (OpenSSH 10.3p1 uplift) is being iterated on by Andrew and Alex. There are some build failures; the plan appears to be addressing them partially in this PR and partially in a follow-up.

## OQS-BoringSSL, oqs-demos

No activity reported.

## Language wrappers

Douglas plans to spend some time on the wrappers — especially the Python wrapper, since he knows Python — once he's pushed liboqs forward a bit more. A number of the open PRs are obsolete (e.g., updates to liboqs 0.14 / 0.15 that will be superseded by 0.16). He suggested triaging the rest with Claude's help.

Michael cautioned against using Claude to close out PRs where there is an external contributor behind them: doing so closes off an opportunity to bring those contributors into the project. He suggested reaching back out to the original PR authors to invite them to push the work forward. Douglas agreed, with the caveat that OQS should do a little work first to demonstrate engagement before asking contributors to re-engage.

## Next meetings

Next OQS status meeting: Tuesday, May 19, 2026, at 12:30 PM US Eastern. Next OQS TSC meeting: Tuesday, June 2, 2026, at 12:30 PM US Eastern.
